title: Chapter 25: Epilogue — The New World
content: Six months later.
The sky was still violet. That hadn't changed. But the Nether-Grid had.

Blackwood University was no longer just a campus. It was the capital of a new territory — the Vance Demesne, as the Exchange had started calling it. Roxanne had expanded her borders to include the hospital, the water treatment plant, the library, and a twelve-block radius of what had once been downtown. All of it was neutral ground. No debts. No collections. No enforcement.

People came from miles away. Families. Survivors. People who had been hiding in basements for half a year, afraid to emerge. They found a city with working water, electricity, food distribution, and a medical clinic that didn't ask for payment.

Lena had published her first book: a collection of sketches documenting the first month of the apocalypse. It was selling for five Obols a copy at the Exchange. She was already working on the second volume.

The Executioner had become the campus's unofficial mascot. Parents warned their children not to wander too close to it. The Executioner didn't mind. It stood at the main gate every day, silent and still, and occasionally wrote messages in the dust for passersby to read.
*HAVE A NICE DAY. — MANAGEMENT.*
Silas had mastered latte art. He could now produce a perfect swan, a rose, and — Roxanne's personal favorite — a tiny depiction of the Primordial Council being hit by a meteor.
As for Roxanne:
She sat in the dean's office — her office — looking at the black card. It still pulsed. It still offered ∞. But she hadn't used it in three weeks.
"You're not spending," Silas observed, setting a latte beside her.
"I don't need to. Everything I wanted to buy, I bought."
"The campus. The hospital. The Enforcement Division."
"The freedom of about twelve thousand people."
Silas considered this. "That was your best purchase."
"I know."
She pocketed the card.
"What now?" he asked.
Roxanne looked out the window at the violet sky, at the campus that was now a city, at the strange new world she had built.
"Now I finish my art portfolio."
Silas raised an eyebrow.
"The apocalypse delayed my graduation. I have a thesis to complete."

"You're the Second Seat of the Primordial Council, a rogue economic reformer who dismantled the Nether-Grid's debt system, and the sole holder of the Primordial Black Card. And you're worried about your thesis?"
"The thesis is about the intersection of Renaissance composition techniques and post-apocalyptic aesthetic theory. It's actually very relevant to my current situation."
Silas looked at her for a long moment. Then he smiled.
"You are, without question, the most absurd being I have ever encountered in four billion years of existence."
"Thank you."
"That was a compliment."
"I know."
She picked up her latte.
"Let's go see what the demon market is selling today. I heard they have a new line of shadow-fabric that would be perfect for my next piece."
"You're going shopping."
"I'm always shopping. I have an infinite card."
He offered his arm. She took it.
Side by side, they walked out into the violet morning.
*THE END*
A week later, the campus had become a city.

Refugees had built homes in the dormitories. The hospital was running at full capacity. The library had been converted into a community center. Lena had opened an art school for the children — "Foundation skills," she called it. "Every civilization needs artists."

Roxanne walked through the streets of her new city. People nodded to her. Some waved. A group of children ran past, chasing a ball made of bundled rags. One of them stopped and stared at the Executioner, who stood motionless at a street corner.
"Is that a monster?" the child asked.
"That's Security," Roxanne said. "He works for me."
"Can I pet him?"
"I wouldn't recommend it."
The child considered this. Then waved at the Executioner. "Bye, Security!"
The Executioner tilted its head. Just slightly.
It raised its chainsaw arm. Gave a tiny, awkward wave back.
Roxanne stared.
"Did it just —"
"It's learning," Silas said, appearing beside her. "You've given it a purpose beyond violence. That changes things."
"I gave it a job."
"To a being that was created only to kill, you gave a job that involves guarding children. That's not a job. That's redemption."
She watched the Executioner lower its arm. The children had already run off, chasing their ball.
"Can entities like that be redeemed?"
"I don't know. But you're giving them the chance to try."
She thought about that.
"I guess that's what the card is for."
"What?"
"Not buying things. Buying people the chance to choose differently."
Silas was quiet for a moment. Then he said, very softly:
"That's what you did for me."
She looked at him.
"I bought a god."
"You bought me freedom. And then you gave me a coffee machine. That's not the same thing."
She smiled.
"Come on. There's a new fabric shop in the Exchange. I heard they're selling shadow-silk from the Eighth Layer."
"You're going shopping again."
"I'm always shopping. I have an infinite card."
He offered his arm. She took it.
Side by side, they walked out into the violet morning.
*THE END*
Two weeks later, the first shipment of textbooks arrived at the campus library.

Not Nether-Grid texts. Art history texts. Roxanne had placed an order through the Exchange for every surviving pre-apocalypse art book she could find. The delivery came via a portal that opened in the main quad, depositing three hundred kilograms of books in neat, labeled stacks.
Lena nearly cried.
"You bought art books?"
"I bought every art book that survived. The Exchange had a warehouse full of them. No one had bid on them because nobody cares about art during an apocalypse."
"But you did."
"Civilization isn't just food and water. It's also what makes life worth living."
She picked up a monograph on Renaissance composition techniques. It was the same book she'd been referencing for her thesis before the world ended.
"I'm starting classes next week," she said.
"Classes?"
"Art history. Monday, Wednesday, Friday. Three PM. In the old lecture hall. I'm teaching."
Lena stared at her.
"You're starting a university in the middle of the apocalypse."
"The campus was a university before the apocalypse. I'm just reopening it."
"With you as the professor."
"Someone has to teach. The other art history professor is currently a pile of ash somewhere."
Lena laughed. Then stopped. Then started sketching the scene — Roxanne standing in a pile of books, the violet sky behind her, the Executioner visible in the distance, guarding the gate.
"This is going to be one of my best drawings," she said.
"Good. I want a copy for my office."
